message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec id="s21">
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The efficacy of the BNT162b2 vaccine in pediatrics was assessed by randomized trials before the Omicron variant’s emergence. The long-term durability of vaccine protection in this population during the Omicron period remains limited.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s22">
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To assess the effectiveness of BNT162b2 in preventing infection and severe diseases with various strains of the SARS-CoV-2 virus in previously uninfected children and adolescents.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s23">
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Comparative effectiveness research accounting for underreported vaccination in three study cohorts: adolescents (12 to 20 years) during the Delta phase, children (5 to 11 years) and adolescents (12 to 20 years) during the Omicron phase.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s24">
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A national collaboration of pediatric health systems (PEDSnet).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s25">
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>77,392 adolescents (45,007 vaccinated) in the Delta phase, 111,539 children (50,398 vaccinated) and 56,080 adolescents (21,180 vaccinated) in the Omicron period.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s26">
                  <jats:title>Exposures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>First dose of the BNT162b2 vaccine vs. no receipt of COVID-19 vaccine.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s27">
                  <jats:title>Measurements</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Outcomes of interest include documented infection, COVID-19 illness severity, admission to an intensive care unit (ICU), and cardiac complications. The effectiveness was reported as (1-relative risk)*100% with confounders balanced via propensity score stratification.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s28">
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>During the Delta period, the estimated effectiveness of BNT162b2 vaccine was 98.4% (95% CI, 98.1 to 98.7) against documented infection among adolescents, with no significant waning after receipt of the first dose. An analysis of cardiac complications did not find an increased risk after vaccination. During the Omicron period, the effectiveness against documented infection among children was estimated to be 74.3% (95% CI, 72.2 to 76.2). Higher levels of effectiveness were observed against moderate or severe COVID-19 (75.5%, 95% CI, 69.0 to 81.0) and ICU admission with COVID-19 (84.9%, 95% CI, 64.8 to 93.5). Among adolescents, the effectiveness against documented Omicron infection was 85.5% (95% CI, 83.8 to 87.1), with 84.8% (95% CI, 77.3 to 89.9) against moderate or severe COVID-19, and 91.5% (95% CI, 69.5 to 97.6)) against ICU admission with COVID-19. The effectiveness of the BNT162b2 vaccine against the Omicron variant declined after 4 months following the first dose and then stabilized. The analysis revealed a lower risk of cardiac complications in the vaccinated group during the Omicron variant period.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s29">
                  <jats:title>Limitations</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Observational study design and potentially undocumented infection.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s30">
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our study suggests that BNT162b2 was effective for various COVID-19-related outcomes in children and adolescents during the Delta and Omicron periods, and there is some evidence of waning effectiveness over time.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec id="s31">
                  <jats:title>Primary Funding Source</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>National Institutes of Health</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Real-world Effectiveness of BNT162b2 Against Infection and Severe Diseases in Children and Adolescents
